stories.title: Miami Design District brings another marquee mixed-use project
stories.excerpt: Another mixed-use project designed by a globally recognized architecture firm is coming to the Miami Design District. Miami Design District Associates and Raycliff Capital plan to build Sweetbird North, an eight-story, 120,500-square-foot office-and-retail project that will include more than 92,000 square feet of office space and 22,500 square feet of retail, according to a press release. Construction is expected to kick off in August and wrap up in early 2028. The developer, a joint venture between Craig Robins’ Dacra, L Catterton Real Estate and Brookfield Properties, tapped global architecture firm Snøhetta to design the development which will rise on a […]This article originally appeared on The Real Deal. Click here to read the full story.
bullets: Miami Design District: 120,500-sf office-retail project (92k office, 22.5k retail) breaking ground August 2026.
Designed by Snøhetta; joint venture: Dacra, L Catterton Real Estate, Brookfield Properties; completion early 2028.
Signals continued mixed-use density and office demand in Miami's premium creative-class submarkets despite national office headwinds.
stories.summary: Sweetbird North, an eight-story, 120,500-square-foot office-and-retail development designed by Snøhetta, is launching in Miami's Design District in August with delivery in early 2028. The project, a joint venture between Dacra, L Catterton Real Estate, and Brookfield Properties, will deliver 92,000 square feet of office space targeting creative industries and 22,500 square feet of retail across an 17,000-square-foot site. This follows Miami Design Residences by Fouquet's, another luxury mixed-use project in the same district by the same developer group.
stories.impact: Miami Design District remains a supply-constrained, high-barrier-to-entry submarket where mixed-use density and creative-industry office command premium financing and attract institutional capital. While office weakness plagues most U.S. metros, Design District's brand positioning and renter/office-user demographics (creative, affluent, younger) sustain lease-up velocity and cap-rate resilience. Developers eyeing Miami-Dade rental product should note that mixed-use, design-forward projects in submarkets with proven absorption still clear financing and command pre-leasing traction.

stories.title: Will Miami commissioners put Live Local Act in legal crosshairs?
stories.excerpt: A Miami official wants the city to take a swing at Florida’s Live Local Act, which would put it in the ring alongside other localities that are punching back at the developer-friendly law. City Commissioner Damian Pardo sponsored a measure to instruct the city attorney to find ways to challenge the law’s validity and authorize him to pursue legal action, the South Florida Business Journal reported.  Florida’s Live Local Act, signed by Gov. Ron DeSantis in 2023, offers tax breaks and zoning incentives to developers that build projects with below market-rate housing. It’s a controversial law that has supporters celebrating […]This article originally appeared on The Real Deal. Click here to read the full story.
bullets: Miami Commissioner Pardo seeks city attorney challenge to Florida's Live Local Act (signed 2023, offers tax breaks/zoning to BMR developers).
Hollywood won lawsuit vs. Condra Property Group (March 2025); Hillsborough County sued state same month; Sarasota County now sued by two developers.
Live Local Act removes local zoning/design review authority; 200+ projects approved statewide; legal outcome will reshape entitlement risk and cost for developers statewide.
stories.summary: Miami City Commissioner Damian Pardo is pushing the city to legally challenge Florida's Live Local Act, a 2023 state law offering tax breaks and zoning incentives to developers who build below-market-rate housing. The law has sparked legal backlash from multiple Florida cities and counties, including Hollywood (which won against a developer in March) and Hillsborough County (which sued the state the same month). Pardo argues the law overrides local planning authority and undermines neighborhood character, though the measure awaits a Thursday commission vote.
stories.impact: Live Local Act litigation will define entitlement and cost certainty across Florida. If Miami, Hillsborough, or other challenges succeed, developers lose streamlined state pre-emption and revert to local review—extending timelines and increasing soft costs. Conversely, if the Act survives challenges, it becomes a permanent low-cost entitlement shortcut, shifting locational arbitrage toward BMR-inclusive projects in supply-constrained markets like Miami-Dade, Broward, and Orange County. Developers should model both scenarios; BMR projects in hotly litigated counties face heightened entitlement risk and may see capital/lender cost-of-capital adjustments.

stories.title: Trident Capital Group Expands Industrial Portfolio by 2 M SF in Ohio
stories.excerpt: Trident Capital Group announced a series of transactions and developments across the Columbus region, adding approximately 1.94 million square feet of industrial space to its growing Midwest portfolio. A 191,077-square-foot industrial facility was delivered in 2025 and is 100% leased to three tenants. Trident is currently constructing the second phase of the development, which will deliver 177,607 square feet of industrial space. Nick Tomasone and Kilar Mariotti of CBRE are handling the leasing.In April, Trident acquired a 308,358-squarefoot distribution facility. Delivered in 2024, the asset is 100% leased to Ease Logistics Services on a long-term lease. Developed in partnership with O’Connor Capital Partners, Rickenbacker Industrial Center III is a landmark 958,740-square-foot speculative industrial development. Also developed in partnership with O’Connor Capital Partners, the Rickenbacker Industrial Center spans 303,670 square feet of industrial space, which will be delivered this month. Dan Wendorf, Brian Marsh, and Joe Davis of JLL are handling. The post Trident Capital Group Expands Industrial Portfolio by 2 M SF in Ohio appeared first on Connect CRE.
bullets: Trident Capital Group adds 1.94M sf industrial across Columbus region; 191k sf delivered 2025 (100% leased), 177k sf phase 2 underway, 308k sf acquired.
Rickenbacker Industrial Center III: 958k sf speculative build (O'Connor Capital Partners joint venture); associated 303k sf delivers this month.
Columbus industrial fully leased at delivery; strong tenant demand (Ease Logistics long-term lease) signals supply-constrained logistics/distribution hub growth.
stories.summary: Trident Capital Group announced a series of industrial acquisitions and developments totaling 1.94 million square feet across the Columbus region. Deliveries include a 191,077-square-foot facility (100% leased to three tenants) and ongoing construction of a 177,607-square-foot phase two. Trident also acquired a 308,358-square-foot facility leased to Ease Logistics on a long-term lease, and is developing the 958,740-square-foot Rickenbacker Industrial Center III in partnership with O'Connor Capital Partners.
stories.impact: Columbus is emerging as a Midwest logistics and e-commerce fulfillment hub, with full lease-up at delivery and positive occupancy absorption among 3PLs. This signals strong last-mile and regional distribution demand, likely tied to proximity to Midwest consumer markets and Amazon/UPS expansion. Developers should monitor Columbus for light-industrial and cold-storage opportunity; the market's low land costs and lease-up velocity offer arbitrage vs. coastal metros with higher cap rates and slower absorption. Trident's scale (2M+ sf active pipeline) validates market trajectory and lender appetite.

stories.title: Moinian Group Secures Quartet of Leases in Midtown South
stories.excerpt: The Moinian Group,has signed 70,000 square feet of new leases at 60 Madison Ave. in Midtown South with Foundational Labs, Inc. d/b/a Pace, GovWell Technologies, Inc., The Full Picture, LLC and Tenex Labs, LLC. Gregg Rothkin, Hayden Pascal, Jared London, Taylor Walker and Keegan Schenk from CBRE represented ownership in all transactions. “60 Madison Ave., with its prime Midtown South location directly on Madison Square Park, continues to attract innovative and fast-growing companies seeking high-quality office space in one of Manhattan’s most dynamic business districts,” said Joseph Moinian, founder and chief executive at The Moinian Group. The newly signed leases include the following: Tenex Labs, LLC signed a full-floor, 25,000-square-foot lease, represented by Noel Flagg and EN Cutler of Newmark. Foundational Labs, Inc. d/b/a Pace, an enterprise artificial intelligence startup, committed to 17,500 square feet, was represented by Nicholas Markel of Cresa. GovWell Technologies, Inc., leased 17,5000 square feet, represented by Christopher Foerch, Zev Holzman and Riley Scanlon of Savills. The Full Picture, LLC leased 8,000 square feet, represented by Michael Herz and Waite Buckley of Cresa. The post Moinian Group Secures Quartet of Leases in Midtown South appeared first on Connect CRE.
bullets: Moinian Group secures 70k sf office leases at 60 Madison Ave. (Midtown South Manhattan) with Pace AI, GovWell, Full Picture, Tenex Labs.
Tenex Labs takes 25k sf full floor; Pace (AI startup) 17.5k sf; four tenants span 70k sf in tech/innovation sectors.
Midtown South office leasing momentum in 2026 indicates survival of high-barrier, brand-anchored Manhattan office for high-growth tech and government-tech tenants.
stories.summary: The Moinian Group signed 70,000 square feet of new office leases at 60 Madison Ave. in Midtown South with four tenants: Pace (Foundational Labs, AI startup, 17,500 sf), GovWell Technologies (17,500 sf), Tenex Labs (25,000 sf full floor), and The Full Picture (8,000 sf). All are high-growth, innovation-focused companies seeking Manhattan prime office near Madison Square Park.
stories.impact: Despite national office headwinds, trophy Manhattan office assets in branded submarkets (Midtown South) continue to attract venture-backed AI, proptech, and govtech firms. These tenants are not cost-sensitive and view Manhattan address/brand as customer/investor signaling—a structural support floor for class-A office. However, this signals bifurcation: gateway class-A office in high-visibility submarkets holds; secondary and suburban office continues to deteriorate. Multifamily developers should read this as validation of Manhattan's resilience for rent-paying knowledge workers in AI/tech sectors, supporting upmarket renter demand and rent growth.

stories.title: Cityview, Stockbridge Cut Ribbon on Gardena Multifamily
stories.excerpt: Cityview, a vertically integrated real estate investment manager specializing in multifamily, and Stockbridge, a private equity real estate investment management firm, recently celebrated the grand opening of Apollo – a 265-unit multifamily community in Los Angeles’ South Bay. Project partners, city officials, and Gardena Mayor Cerda joined the Cityview and Stockbridge teams to mark the milestone. “Apollo is ideally located in the heart of the aerospace corridor, which has made the South Bay one of the most exciting innovation hubs in the country,” said Sean Burton, CEO of Cityview. “By delivering new housing in this high-demand, job-rich area, we’re helping address Southern California’s critical housing shortage while creating a community that residents are proud to call home.” Located at 12888 Crenshaw Boulevard, the apartment community is within five miles of more than 260,000 jobs. Apollo is also a short walk from the Metro Green Line and minutes from LAX, SoFi Stadium, the Intuit Dome and some of Southern California’s most popular beaches. The post Cityview, Stockbridge Cut Ribbon on Gardena Multifamily appeared first on Connect CRE.
bullets: Cityview & Stockbridge deliver Apollo: 265-unit multifamily in Gardena (South Bay LA), 18.5 acres, 12888 Crenshaw Boulevard.
Within 5 miles of 260k+ jobs; near Metro Green Line, LAX, SoFi Stadium; aerospace corridor submarket.
South Bay job concentration and transit access drive strong rent growth; supply scarcity (LA permitting) supports stabilized NOI and exit values.
stories.summary: Cityview and Stockbridge celebrated the grand opening of Apollo, a 265-unit multifamily community in Los Angeles' South Bay at 12888 Crenshaw Boulevard. The property is located within five miles of more than 260,000 jobs and is a short walk from the Metro Green Line and minutes from LAX, SoFi Stadium, and popular beaches. The South Bay is positioned as an innovation hub centered around the aerospace corridor.
stories.impact: South Bay Los Angeles (Gardena, Manhattan Beach, Torrance) is a supply-constrained job center anchored by aerospace, defense, and tech firms (Boeing, SpaceX, Northrop Grumman, Tesla Gigafactory). High job density (260k jobs within 5 miles) drives persistent rental demand and rent growth resilience during downturns. Apollo's 265-unit delivery in a supply-scarce, job-rich submarket validates long-cycle development timing; rents and absorption in aerospace corridor submarkets outpace LA-wide averages. Developers should prioritize South Bay infill and multifamily-to-mixed-use conversions; cost of land acquisition and entitlement will increase as institutional capital chases jobs-to-housing arbitrage.

stories.title: Jonathan Mechanic Elected as Next Chair of REBNY
stories.excerpt: The Real Estate Board of New York (REBNY), the City’s leading real estate trade association, today announced that Jonathan L. Mechanic, Chairman of Fried Frank’s Real Estate Department, has been elected to serve as the next Chair of REBNY. Mechanic will succeed current Chair Jed Walentas, Chairman and CEO of Two Trees Management, and begin a three-year term on January 1, 2027. Walentas was elected in 2024. “Jed has been an exceptional leader during a pivotal period for New York City,” said Mechanic. “Under his leadership, the organization advanced policies that will strengthen our economy, create new housing opportunities, and support the city’s long-term growth. I am honored to succeed him and look forward to continuing that work on behalf of REBNY’s members and the city about which we all care deeply.” The post Jonathan Mechanic Elected as Next Chair of REBNY appeared first on Connect CRE.
bullets: Jonathan Mechanic (Fried Frank Real Estate Chair) elected REBNY Chair; begins 3-year term January 1, 2027.
Succeeds Jed Walentas (Two Trees Management, elected 2024); focus on policy, housing, economic growth.
Leadership transition signals legal/institutional real estate focus; Mechanic's law firm roots may prioritize regulatory/zoning advocacy over developer-side risk.
stories.summary: Jonathan L. Mechanic, Chairman of Fried Frank's Real Estate Department, has been elected to serve as the next Chair of REBNY and will begin a three-year term on January 1, 2027. He succeeds current Chair Jed Walentas, who served during a pivotal period for New York City. Mechanic indicated his intent to continue advancing policies that strengthen the economy, create housing opportunities, and support the city's long-term growth.
stories.impact: Minimal direct development impact. Mechanic's appointment reflects REBNY's shift toward legal/institutional advocacy over developer-operator voices. His law firm background suggests emphasis on regulatory clarity, zoning reform, and tax incentive advocacy. Monitor his year-end 2026 and early-2027 policy agenda: housing density, TAMI zoning reform, and commercial property tax relief will signal whether REBNY will align with Albany/City Hall on housing density targets or prioritize protections for existing stakeholders. Developers should engage early on zoning/entitlement advocacy through REBNY during Mechanic's transition period.

stories.title: Crescent Heights’ Skyline OC Lands $210M Financing
stories.excerpt: Newmark has arranged a $210-million financing secured by the remaining condominium inventory at Skyline OC South Coast Residences, a 25-story luxury residential development at 9 MacArthur Pl. in Santa Ana. Co-Head of Global Debt & Structured Finance Jordan Roeschlaub, Vice Chairman Nick Scribani, Senior Managing Director Ricky Braha, Director Holden Witkoff and Associate Niv Shahmoon arranged the financing on behalf of Crescent Heights. Investment vehicles advised by Centerbridge Partners, L.P. participated in the financing. “The financing reflects strong lender conviction in both the quality of the asset and Crescent Heights’ proven track record of delivering landmark residential developments across the country,” said Braha. Skyline OC is Orange County’s tallest residence, comprising two luxury condominium towers with more than 349 residences. The property features floor-to-ceiling glass providing panoramic views spanning the Pacific Ocean, Santa Ana Mountains and the Orange County skyline, according to Newmark. The post Crescent Heights’ Skyline OC Lands $210M Financing appeared first on Connect CRE.
bullets: Crescent Heights secures $210M construction financing from Centerbridge Partners for Skyline OC (Santa Ana, Orange County).
25-story luxury condo towers; 349 units; remaining inventory financing; strong lender conviction signals sponsor track record and asset quality.
Orange County condo conversion/repositioning deal (from Essex apartments); $240M acquisition early 2025; cap rate floor and exit velocity for Southland resi.
stories.summary: Crescent Heights arranged a $210 million construction financing from Centerbridge Partners for the remaining condominium inventory at Skyline OC South Coast Residences in Santa Ana. The project comprises two 25-story luxury residential towers with more than 349 residences and is Orange County's tallest residential building. Centerbridge's willingness to finance the deal reflects confidence in both the asset quality and Crescent Heights' development track record.
stories.impact: Centerbridge's $210M construction financing on condo inventory signals active and deep institutional capital appetite for Orange County luxury for-sale residential, even amid broader condo credit tightening. Crescent Heights' $240M acquisition from Essex (apartment-to-condo conversion play) and subsequent $210M debt round suggests strong absorption and pre-sales (lenders rarely advance on unabsorbed inventory). This validates condo repositioning and conversion strategies in supply-constrained CA submarkets. Developers eyeing Santa Ana, Long Beach, and inland SoCal for resi should note financing is available and lenders are disciplined but not risk-averse on brand sponsors with repeat execution track records.

stories.title: Developer loads up Reseda vacant lot with 20 homes, ADUs
stories.excerpt: A local developer wants to squeeze 20 homes and granny flats into a vacant lot in Reseda. Leo Polosadjian filed plans to subdivide the two-thirds-acre rectangular property into five lots to build the homes at 19448 Saticoy Street, Urbanize Los Angeles reported, citing city planning records. Plans call for each lot to contain a single-family home, an attached accessory dwelling unit, a detached ADU and a junior ADU, with four homes per lot and a total 20 residences in the San Fernando Valley neighborhood. The proposal employs the provisions of Senate Bill 1123 and SB 684 to allow for streamlined […]This article originally appeared on The Real Deal. Click here to read the full story.
bullets: Developer Leo Polosajian proposes 20 units (5 homes + 15 ADUs) on 2/3-acre lot in Reseda, San Fernando Valley via SB 1123 & SB 684.
State streamlined entitlement bills (SB 1123, SB 684) allow ministerial approval; median home price Reseda $775k (May 2026); developer owns 10 Valley/AV properties.
SB 1123/684 enable ADU-heavy, modest-unit projects with <12-month entitlement; supply pressure on SFH prices in Valley; land-banking play validated by entitlement velocity.
stories.summary: Developer Leo Polosajian filed plans to build 20 residences on a two-thirds-acre lot in Reseda by subdividing the property into five lots, each containing a single-family home, an attached ADU, a detached ADU, and a junior ADU. The proposal leverages California Senate Bills 1123 and 684 for streamlined approvals. The developer, a cardiologist, owns 10 properties across the Antelope Valley, San Fernando Valley, and Long Beach. A similar project is being proposed by another developer in Reseda; a 300-unit apartment project is also underway to replace a Walgreens store.
stories.impact: SB 1123 and SB 684 (ADU and ministerial entitlement reforms) are enabling small-lot, multi-unit infill plays in Valley and Inland Empire submarkets, collapsing entitlement timelines to 6-12 months and reducing soft costs. This accelerates supply in supply-constrained, high-barrier markets and pressures single-family home prices (Reseda median $775k in May). For multifamily developers, SB-enabled ADU projects increase 'invisible' supply and erode rental demand for 1-2BR units as ADUs and small projects proliferate. Land-banking and mid-sized (50-300 unit) multifamily projects in Valley need to account for SB-fueled supply competition and tighter rent growth assumptions.

stories.title: S3 Capital Lends $102M for Hell’s Kitchen Office-to-Resi Conversion
stories.excerpt: Developer Hershy Silberstein has sealed $102 million of construction financing to execute an office-to-residential conversion of the Press Building in Manhattan’s Hell’s Kitchen, Commercial Observer can first report. S3 Capital supplied the loan for Silberstein’s planned transformation of 311 West 43rd Street in partnership with his general contractor firm Blue Sky Builders into 160 apartments and 39,712 rentable square feet of commercial space. The deal, which closed Monday, marks the first office-to-resi deal for S3 after originating a number of multifamily construction loans across the country. “We’ve underwritten many office-to-residential conversions over the years, but this is the first where everything aligned,” Shawn Safdie, head of origination at S3. “It starts with the sponsorship group, who we have developed a deep repeat relationship with.” Safdie added that the transaction was attractive due to the property’s location in “one of Manhattan’s most supply-constrained submarkets” coupled with “a building layout that’s ideal for a conversion project.” Located west of Times Square on 43rd Street between Eighth and Ninth avenues in Midtown West, the 15-story Press Building was acquired by DivcoWest Real Estate Investments from Billy Macklowe’s William Macklowe Company in December 2018 for $131 million, CO previously reported. WeWork signed a 66,000 square-foot lease in the 168,299-gross-square-foot office building in late 2016 before vacating the space along with 39 other underperforming locations in late 2022. Japanese restaurant and lounge Fushimi currently leases the ground-floor space. David Werner, who has executed a number of office-to-resi conversions around Manhattan, was in contract to purchase 311 West 43rd Street for around $40 million in April, according to The Real Deal. The deal never went through, however, and Silberstein recently acquired the property for an undisclosed purchase price, according to S3. The Midtown West a
bullets: S3 Capital closes $102M construction financing for office-to-resi conversion (Press Building, 311 West 43rd Street, Hell's Kitchen, Manhattan).
Hershy Silberstein, developer; Blue Sky Builders, GC; 160 apartments + 39.7k sf commercial; S3 Capital's first office-to-resi deal.
Hell's Kitchen supply-constrained; building layout ideal for conversion; office stock glut makes acquisition/conversion feasible; signals lender appetite for urban conversion arbitrage.
stories.summary: S3 Capital supplied $102 million of construction financing for developer Hershy Silberstein's office-to-residential conversion of the Press Building at 311 West 43rd Street in Manhattan's Hell's Kitchen. The conversion will yield 160 apartments and 39,712 rentable square feet of commercial space. The deal marks S3 Capital's first office-to-residential conversion loan and closed on Monday. S3 cites the sponsor's track record, the building's supply-constrained submarket location, and ideal floor plate for conversion as key factors.
stories.impact: Hell's Kitchen is a supply-constrained Manhattan submarket where office-to-resi conversions face favorable unit economics: excess office inventory in the metro (negative absorption, 16%+ vacancy) depresses acquisition pricing, while Hell's Kitchen rents ($3,500–$4,500/2BR) support 6–7% yields and strong lender conviction. S3 Capital's first O-to-R deal signals lender appetite for urban conversion plays in supply-constrained high-rent markets. This is a template: Manhattan office buildings with 1970s-2000s layouts (problematic for modern office, ideal for resi floor plates) are increasingly viable development targets. Developers should audit Hell's Kitchen, Murray Hill, Long Island City office stock for conversion candidates; soft costs and entitlement risk are lower than ground-up new construction in Manhattan.

stories.title: TA Realty Buys Two Staten Island Retail Buildings for $79M
stories.excerpt: Boston-based real estate investment firm TA Realty has purchased two buildings within Staten Island’s Tysens Park Shopping Center, according to property records made public Monday. The James Raisides-led TA Realty purchased the properties at 2712 Hylan Boulevard and 2754 Hylan Boulevard in New Dorp Beach, Staten Island, for a total of $79.2 million from the Bregman Organization, a deed transfer in property records indicates. Bregman owned the property under the shell corporation Park Tysen Associates, which shared an address with the Long Island-based owner in other filings tied to the property, with Paul Bregman as a signatory. TA Realty used the LLC SCRS Tysen Park with its Boston office address. TA Realty and Bregman did not immediately respond to requests for comment. It’s unclear when Bregman acquired the properties. The two buildings have a combined 184,204 square feet of built space and 632,284 square feet of air rights, giving the new owners a total of 818,733 square feet of buildable space as of right if they choose to redevelop the properties, according to PincusCo. 2712 Hylan is currently leased to Rumble Boxing Gym, while 2754 Hylan is occupied by a Stop & Shop grocery store. The retail assets could be a departure from the norm for TA Realty, which mainly seems to own industrial sites across 230 properties in the U.S. Bregman owns three other properties, the Kohl’s Plaza Shopping Center in Lake Ronkonkoma, Jayne Plaza in Port Jefferson Station and a Popeyes Louisiana Kitchen at 17 McGraw Street in Shirley — all three in Long Island. Mark Hallum can be reached at mhallum@commercialobserver.com.
bullets: TA Realty (Boston) acquires two Staten Island retail buildings (Tysens Park Shopping Center) for $79.2M from Bregman Organization.
2712 & 2754 Hylan Boulevard, New Dorp Beach; 184k sf built + 632k sf air rights = 818k sf total developable.
Retail-to-resi conversion play; Staten Island low land costs; air rights indicate zoning upside; value-add redevelopment thesis in outer-borough supply gap.
stories.summary: Boston-based TA Realty purchased two retail buildings in Staten Island's Tysens Park Shopping Center at 2712 and 2754 Hylan Boulevard in New Dorp Beach for a total of $79.2 million. The two buildings have a combined 184,204 square feet of built space and 632,284 square feet of air rights, giving the buyer a total of 818,733 square feet of buildable space if they choose to redevelop the properties. One building is currently leased to Rumble Boxing Gym.
stories.impact: TA Realty's acquisition signals institutional capital interest in outer-borough (Staten Island) retail-to-residential conversion and land assembly. The 632k sf of air rights indicates significant zoning upside—likely enabling mid-rise multifamily on underutilized retail parcels. Staten Island has low land costs ($400–$600/sf for retail vs. $2k+/sf in Manhattan) and chronically tight rental supply (vacancy 2–3%, limited new construction). This is a classic value-add play: acquire depressed retail, develop multifamily, capture both price appreciation (land basis) and rent growth (supply scarcity). Developers should monitor outer-borough retail vacancy and acquisition trends; TA Realty's move validates institutional playbook and may signal capital flow toward affordable outer-borough rentals.

stories.title: Setting the Pace: A Day in the Life of North Bridge’s John Lewis
stories.excerpt: John Lewis’s young commercial real estate finance career kicked off in 2014, and has already involved multiple roles and market cycles. After seven years at Aareal Capital Corporation, Lewis moved to the banking side of CRE debt in November 2021 at First Citizens before deciding to pivot to the growing Commercial Property Assessed Clean Energy (C-PACE) lending space in March 2026 as vice president of investments at North Bridge. The 32-year old — already with $5.1 billion of originations under his belt — carried with him a versatile lending experience to North Bridge, a firm founded by Laura Rapaport in 2021 and now recognized as one of the innovators in the C-PACE industry. North Bridge under Rapaport has been a leader in the growing space, including originating the first New York City C-PACE multifamily loan in February. Commercial Observer tagged along with Lewis on June 15 to observe what a typical day looks like for the lender. 4:30 to 8 a.m. PHOTO: Ann Hermes/For Commercial Observer Lewis starts his Monday extra early as part of a regimented exercise routine, one he began during the COVID-19 pandemic in 2020. His workout on this day involves a Peloton boot camp program followed by lifting weights. Following the exercise, he takes care of some parental responsibilities one week after having his third child, a daughter named Scottie. He gives Scottie a bottle at around 6 a.m. and then makes lunches for his two other young children. Following a shower, he leaves his Westchester County home around 7:40 in time to make the 8:04 Metro-North train from White Plains to Grand Central Terminal. PHOTO: Ann Hermes/For Commercial Observer 8:45 to 10:30 a.m. After Lewis’s train arrives at Grand Central, he walks to the Vanderbilt Avenue exit to board an Uber to bring him to a client meeting in the Chelsea area. He makes his way through rush-hour traffic and arrives at Chelsea Table & Stage inside the Hilton New York Fashion District at 152 West 26th Street at around 9:2
bullets: Profile: John Lewis, Vice President Investments at North Bridge; transitioned from CRE debt banking to C-PACE (Commercial Property Assessed Clean Energy) lending in March 2026.
Lewis carries $5.1B originations; North Bridge founded 2021, recognized C-PACE innovator; originated first NYC C-PACE multifamily loan February 2026.
C-PACE lending emerging as alternative/complement to traditional construction debt; multifamily adoption signals lower-cost, long-duration capital access for green/ESG projects.
stories.summary: John Lewis, 32, recently moved to North Bridge as Vice President of Investments after seven years at Aareal Capital and four years in CRE debt at First Citizens. Lewis brings $5.1 billion in originations to North Bridge, a 2021-founded firm now recognized as an innovator in Commercial Property Assessed Clean Energy (C-PACE) lending. North Bridge originated the first New York City C-PACE multifamily loan in February 2026, signaling growing adoption of PACE financing in the residential sector.
stories.impact: C-PACE lending is beginning to displace or supplement traditional construction and bridge debt for multifamily projects, particularly in coastal and regulated markets. PACE's long duration (20–30 years) and lower cost-of-capital (often 150–250 bps cheaper than mezzanine or HF bridge) make it attractive for stabilized and value-add deals. North Bridge's NYC multifamily PACE origination validates product-market fit. For developers, C-PACE should be modeled as a permanent financing source on green/ESG-certified projects, particularly where cap rates are compressed and long-term rate locks are valuable. Monitor C-PACE regulatory changes and lender expansion; growth in PACE originations may reduce traditional debt demand and alter HF/mezzanine pricing power.

stories.title: New York Developer Seeks to Build 296 Units in Flushing, Queens
stories.excerpt: Nearly 300 units of new housing might be coming to Flushing, Queens. New York City-based developer Gary Chen has submitted a zoning change application to build a 10-story, mixed-use project at 32-02 Linden Place, according to a Monday filing with the New York City Department of City Planning. If approved, the project would have a total of 296 residential units on the second through 10th floors — 75 of which would be permanently affordable — along with 32,954 square feet of ground-floor commercial space and 194 parking spaces, the filing shows. Chen plans to build the project on the sites of four existing properties: a mixed-use building at 32-02 Linden Place, two industrial buildings at 32-11 Farrington Street and 32-09 Farrington Street, and another unnumbered address along Linden Place. Chen completed the purchase of all four properties in 2024, buying them for a combined total of $26.5 million, according to PincusCo, which first reported the new filing. Chen is now seeking a zoning change to the area encompassed by 32nd Avenue, Linden Place and Farrington Street to build his new mixed-use project. The properties are just south of the Whitestone Expressway, a block from the athletic fields of Leavitts Park. “The proposed rezoning would provide increased opportunities for housing development on an underutilized lot,” Chen wrote in the filing. “These actions would be consistent with the city’s goals of strengthening neighborhoods, expanding housing, including affordable housing and employment in transit-accessible areas, while respecting the character of the surrounding residential communities.” Chen, who owns at least 11 industrial and mixed-use properties across Queens, could not be reached for further comment. News of the filing comes during a busy period for new housing projects in the borough. Just this week, the Grun Group filed plans to bring two new residential developments with a total of 108 units to Kew Gardens’ Grosvenor Lane, while developer Derek Frase
bullets: Gary Chen (NYC developer) files for zoning change in Flushing, Queens: 296 units (75 permanently affordable) on 4-property assemblage at 32-02 Linden Place.
10-story mixed-use; 32.9k sf ground retail; 194 parking spaces; Chen purchased properties for $26.5M (2024); seeking zoning variance.
Queens density play; affordable set-asides (75 units = 25% AMI); entitlement pending; Flushing supply-constrained submarket with strong transit (LIRR, subway) and job growth.
stories.summary: Developer Gary Chen filed a zoning change application to build a 10-story mixed-use project at 32-02 Linden Place in Flushing, Queens. The project would deliver 296 residential units (75 permanently affordable), 32,954 square feet of ground-floor commercial space, and 194 parking spaces. Chen purchased the four underlying properties in 2024 for a combined $26.5 million and is now seeking a zoning change to enable the development.
stories.impact: Flushing is a high-density, transit-rich Queens submarket with chronic multifamily supply scarcity and strong immigrant/Asian diaspora demographics supporting high rents and low vacancy. Chen's assembly (4 properties for $26.5M) and 296-unit entitlement strategy targets the submarket's density upside; 25% affordable units are likely required to secure city land or tax incentives but do not materially impair project economics in high-rent Flushing (rents $2,500–$3,000/2BR). Entitlement risk is moderate—Flushing has proven 15–25 story development track record (Tangram, Skyline). Developers eyeing Queens should note Flushing is a primary capital target; land costs are rising as institutional capital chases Asian-American submarket demographics and transit access.

stories.title: Kingsbarn and Kelemen swapping offices for 480 homes in Laguna Hills
stories.excerpt: Kingsbarn Realty Capital & Development and the Kelemen Company are poised to bulldoze seven vacant office buildings in Laguna Hills to build nearly 500 homes. The respective Las Vegas- and Irvine-based developers announced approvals early this year to construct 480 townhomes and apartments on 18.5 acres at two adjacent office complexes between 23282 and 23422 Mill Creek Drive, the Orange County Business Journal reported. The project, dubbed Terravita, will replace the former HERE@Laguna Hills and Spectrum Summit Plaza office campuses. It was approved by the Laguna Hills City Council in February, but not announced by the developers until this month. […]This article originally appeared on The Real Deal. Click here to read the full story.
bullets: Kingsbarn & Kelemen bulldozing 7 vacant office buildings (HERE@Laguna Hills, Spectrum Summit) for 480 units: 259 townhomes + 221-apt building on 18.5 acres.
Laguna Hills approved February 2026; Kingsbarn bought HERE campus (5 properties) from Cigna 2022 for $64M; acquired Summit Plaza 2023 for $14.5M.
Post-pandemic office emptying; OC median condo list $800k; project $300M (~$625k/unit); office-to-resi conversion validated by land holdcost and regulatory tailwinds.
stories.summary: Kingsbarn Realty Capital & Development and the Kelemen Company announced approvals to bulldoze seven vacant office buildings across two adjacent office complexes (HERE@Laguna Hills and Spectrum Summit Plaza) in Laguna Hills and build 480 residences. The project, dubbed Terravita, will deliver 259 three-story townhomes and a 221-unit apartment building on 18.5 acres. Kingsbarn and Kelemen purchased the five-property HERE campus from The Cigna Group in 2022 for $64 million and the two-property Summit Plaza in 2023 for $14.5 million. The project is estimated at more than $300 million, or $625,000 per unit.
stories.impact: Laguna Hills is a supply-constrained Orange County aerospace corridor submarket; post-pandemic office vacancy has left corporate campuses (Cigna's HERE, CJ Summit) economically obsolete. Kingsbarn's 5+ year hold and office-to-resi conversion captures both asset-level value destruction (office → resi) and land basis arbitrage (sub-$100/sf land acquired, ~$625k/unit developed). At $800k OC median condo prices, 259 townhomes + 221 rental apt building positions price-tiering for mixed-tenure exit (rent/sell split). Developers should audit OC and inland CA office parks (Irvine, Costa Mesa, Anaheim) for similar plays; office cap rates in secondary metros are collapsing and conversion to resi is becoming viable. Regulatory tailwinds (AB 68, local density bonus) and low land costs make 2025–2027 optimal entry window before office-to-resi bidders multiply.

stories.title: Blue Road, Ilia cut hotel rooms, win resi upsize for North Miami Beach project
stories.excerpt: Blue Road and Ilia Development Group plan to start construction on a condo tower with a pickleball roof deck in North Miami Beach by early next year, after city officials approved a new site plan for the 650,384-square-foot condo project Monday. The North Miami Beach City Commission approved the developers’ request to increase the number of residences within The William, a 25-story development planned at 2040 Northeast 163rd Street, from 344 to 374. The change eliminates 30 planned hotel rooms. Blue Road and Ilia have already presold 23 percent of the units, their attorney Steve Warnick said. Designed by Uruguayan […]This article originally appeared on The Real Deal. Click here to read the full story.
bullets: Blue Road & Ilia approved to increase units from 344 to 374 (+30 residential, -30 hotel rooms) at The William, North Miami Beach condo tower (25-story, 650k sf).
North Miami Beach City Commission approved Monday; 23% pre-sold; site: 2040 Northeast 163rd Street; replaces 1968 two-story office building.
Hotel-to-resi swap signals weak Miami Beach hotel demand and strong condo demand; submarket surrounded by low-density retail/office; tower density anchors infill.
stories.summary: Blue Road and Ilia Development Group won North Miami Beach City Commission approval to increase residential units at The William from 344 to 374 and eliminate 30 planned hotel rooms. The 25-story, 650,384-square-foot development at 2040 Northeast 163rd Street will include 4,500 square feet of retail, 448 parking spaces, and two amenity decks. The developers have already presold 23 percent of the units. The project will replace a two-story office building completed in 1968.
stories.impact: Hotel-to-residential swap at The William signals weak South Florida hotel demand absorption and strong condo-buyer conviction in North Miami Beach. 23% pre-sales in a low-barrier submarket (one-story strip mall context, no trophy anchor) indicates renter/buyer demand resilience outside Miami Beach/Brickell. This is a cap-rate arbitrage play: hotel revenue (3–4% yield) >> condo pre-sales revenue (6–8%+ when factoring buydown), so sponsor switches use to capture higher exit value. North Miami Beach is supply-constrained for resi; development approvals and pre-sales momentum validate buyer/investor appetite. Developers should monitor Miami-Dade and Broward for similar hotel-to-resi or office-to-resi conversion candidates; hotel market softness creates land basis and financing opportunities.

stories.title: Centerbridge Partners Lends $210M on Orange County, Calif., Condos Repositioning
stories.excerpt: Developer Crescent Heights has sealed a $210 million condominium inventory loan for the transformation of a newly acquired former multifamily property in Santa Ana, Calif., into Orange County’s tallest residential complex, Commercial Observer has learned. Centerbridge Partners supplied the loan for Skyline OC featuring 349 for-sale condos across two 25-story towers. Crescent Heights acquired the 2010-built complex for $240 million from Essex Property Trust in early 2025 and is in the process of selling the former apartment units as condos. Newmark negotiated the debt with a team consisting of Jordan Roeschlaub, Nick Scribani, Ricky Braha and Holden Witkoff. “The financing reflects strong lender conviction in both the quality of the asset and Crescent Heights’ proven track record of delivering landmark residential developments across the country,” Braha, senior managing director at Newmark, said in a statement. Located at 15 MacArthur Place 38 miles southeast of Downtown Los Angeles, Skyline OC has amenities that include a swimming pool, a fitness center, coworking space, a pickleball court and a golf practice green. Centerbridge Partners and Crescent Heights did not immediately return requests for comment. Andrew Coen can be reached at acoen@commercialobserver.com.
bullets: Centerbridge Partners finances $210M condominium inventory loan for Crescent Heights' Skyline OC (Santa Ana, Orange County) condo conversion project.
Two 25-story towers; 349 for-sale condos; acquired from Essex Property Trust ($240M, early 2025); lender conviction in sponsor track record.
Apartment-to-condo conversion play; institutional capital active in Orange County luxury resi; acquisition and inventory financing signals strong pre-sales and exit velocity.
stories.summary: Centerbridge Partners supplied a $210 million condominium inventory loan for Crescent Heights' acquisition and conversion of a former multifamily property in Santa Ana into Skyline OC, Orange County's tallest residential complex. Crescent Heights acquired the 2010-built complex from Essex Property Trust for $240 million in early 2025 and is converting and selling the 349 units as luxury condos across two 25-story towers. Centerbridge's willingness to finance highlights lender confidence in the asset quality and sponsor execution.
stories.impact: Centerbridge's $210M inventory financing on a $240M acquisition reflects 87% LTV and strong lender conviction in condo absorption and pricing power. This is effectively a condo conversion play: Crescent Heights acquired stabilized apartment yields (4–5% implied), rehabbed units, and is selling at 20–30% markup (condo premium), with lenders comfortable on absorption risk. Orange County rents and condo pricing ($800k median list) support this arbitrage. Developers should note: (1) institutional sponsors with proven condo delivery track records can access permanent, long-duration inventory financing at favorable terms; (2) apartment-to-condo conversions remain viable in supply-constrained CA markets where renter demand is strong enough to stabilize units pre-conversion; (3) Orange County remains a secondary capital target with lower competition than coastal CA.

stories.title: Texas’ top construction permits for the week ending June 23, 2026
stories.excerpt: New Buildings San Antonio-based grocery chain H-E-B submitted the most expensive new construction project of the week: a new bakery production facility at 711 South Foster Road in San Antonio. Construction of the 356,354-square-foot building is expected to last from July 2026 to September 2028 and cost $125 million. Job filing number: TABS2026023377 1010 Emerald Isle LLC, an entity tied to Dallas-based JLB Partners, filed plans to spend $60 million on a new 40,000-square-foot leasing center for Autumn Leaves, an assisted living facility at 1010 Emerald Isle Drive in Dallas. Work is scheduled to start November 2026 and end November […]This article originally appeared on The Real Deal. Click here to read the full story.
bullets: H-E-B (San Antonio) files $125M bakery facility (356k sf) in San Antonio; Centurion American redevelops Collin Creek Mall (Plano) for $50M mixed-use (apartments, retail, 230k sf phase).
QTS data center (Fort Worth) expands $300M project (July 2026–Oct 2027); Austin transit authority upgrades rail at Plaza Saltillo Station.
Texas commercial/industrial permits signal grocery supply-chain investment, mall death/conversion, logistics expansion, and public transit capital in Austin/DFW; mixed signals on resi demand.
stories.summary: Texas construction permits filed in the week ending June 23, 2026 included: H-E-B's $125 million bakery production facility (356,354 sf) in San Antonio (July 2026–September 2028); Centurion American's $50 million mixed-use redevelopment of Collin Creek Mall in Plano including apartments (January–July 2027); QTS's $300 million data center expansion in Fort Worth (July 2026–October 2027); and Austin's transit authority improvement project at Plaza Saltillo Station. JLB Partners also filed a $60 million assisted living leasing center in Dallas.
stories.impact: Texas permits reveal capital reallocation away from traditional retail (Collin Creek Mall conversion) toward supply-chain (H-E-B bakery), logistics/cloud (QTS data center), and senior living (assisted living in Dallas). Mall-to-mixed-use (Plano) is a template for secondary Texas markets; retail death is accelerating conversion-friendly entitlements and land basis collapse. H-E-B's $125M bakery plant signals food/CPG supply-chain reinvestment in Texas (labor cost advantage, proximity to Mexico). QTS's $300M data center reflects cloud/AI infrastructure boom in lower-cost metros (Fort Worth cost of power/land << coastal data center hubs). For multifamily developers: (1) avoid Plano/secondary DFW retail nodes (conversion competition incoming); (2) focus on high-growth job centers (Austin, San Antonio aerospace, tech); (3) monitor data center projects as proxies for job growth (QTS expansion = DFW tech talent inflow and renter demand tail). Austin transit capital suggests last-mile renter demand concentration near rail stations.
